introduction.p: The conversion to HTML deliberately produces plain, semantic markup and leaves nearly all of the visual presentation to CSS (see for the publisher-facing view). Every HTML book loads a single theme.css file, which is not written by hand but built from SCSS source by a small node script. This chapter is a map of that source, so you can find your way around to adjust an existing theme or contribute a new one. You do not need to be a web developer; a working knowledge of CSS and a willingness to read existing files is enough. SCSS (or Sass) is a superset of CSS that adds variables, nesting, functions, and a module system (@use) for splitting styles across files; the result is still ordinary CSS. The Sass basics guide covers everything you will meet here.

section.label: css-directory
section.title: The css Directory
section.p:
All styling source lives in the top-level css directory, whose README.md is the authoritative reference. The folders are:
targetseach target is a self-contained final product: one entry file that compiles to one CSS file. The modern HTML themes are in targets/html, one folder per theme, named for a city (default-modern, salem, denver, greeley, boulder, tacoma). Non-web targets (epub, kindle, revealjs, the print worksheet) live here too. A file used by only one target belongs in that target's folder.
componentssource shared across targets, grouped by what it styles: chunks (PreTeXt containers like exercises and knowls), elements (small pieces such as figures and lists), page-parts (table of contents, navigation bar, banner), interactives (Sage, Runestone, ), and helpers (reusable mixins). Editing a component affects every target that uses it.
colorscolor palettes a theme can draw on, plus the master list of color variables (see ).
fontsthe mechanism a theme uses to select fonts.
distthe built, ready-to-use CSS files (and source maps). Generated outputnever edit by hand (see ).
legacyplain CSS for older themes that predate the SCSS system, kept for backward compatibility; new work does not go here. other holds CSS that is not part of a book, such as the catalog.

section.label: css-theme-assembly
section.title: How a Theme Is Assembled
section.p: A theme's entry file (for example targets/html/default-modern/theme-default-modern.scss) is short and readable, and does three things: it declares the variables a publisher may override (each marked !default); it pulls in components and a color palette with @use; and it finishes by emitting the color rules. Reading one entry file top to bottom is the fastest way to understand a theme.

The css/README.md describes four ways a theme can differ from another, from most to least separation. Knowing which one a difference uses tells you where to change it.
Alternative files. For substantially different styling, a theme imports one of several variant files (_toc-default versus _toc-overlay), with common parts in a shared file they both import.
SCSS variables. For smaller variations that touch several rules (a margin, rounded corners), a value is passed as an SCSS variable through the imports, with a sensible default.
CSS custom properties. Differences that are only a value (most colors) are emitted as CSS variables like --toc-border-color; a theme need only set a different value.
Theme-level overrides. A theme's entry file can add or override rules after the importsgood for a change that suits only that theme and extends, rather than undoes, the defaults.

Files in targets are owned by their folder; files in components are shared, so test every target that uses a component you edit. Avoid copying large blocks of rules between targetsfactor genuinely reusable rules into a componentbut do not create a component merely to share a handful of rules.

section.label: css-colors
section.title: Colors and Palettes
section.p: Colors are kept separate from layout. colors/_color-vars.scss is the master list of every color, given semantic names (--link-text-color, not --pretty-blue) with defaults. A palette file (such as _palette-dual.scss) starts from that list, overrides the entries it cares about, and produces an SCSS map named $colors. The theme hands that map to the set-root-colors mixin, which emits each entry as a CSS custom property on :rootso a publisher (or a later rule) can override any single color without rebuilding.

A palette typically defines a few named schemes (such as blue-red or greens) and accepts overrides for the primary and secondary colors. Those choices arrive as SCSS variables the build script sets from publisher options (see and the publisher list at ). Helpers in colors/_color-helpers.scss compute tints, shades, and tones (via the CSS color-mix function, so blends track any override), and a theme supplies a separate dark-mode palette emitted under :root.dark-mode.

section.label: css-building
section.title: Building the CSS
section.p: The build script is script/cssbuilder, run with node. In normal use you never invoke it directlythe script and the CLI build whatever theme a document needs. The one-time setup is to install node and run npm install once in script/cssbuilder (the CLI does even this for you). See for node and npm, and for the -c theme shortcut that rebuilds only the theme.

When developing a theme it is convenient to run the script yourself. From script/cssbuilder, npm run build builds everything into css/dist; to work on one theme, build it straight into a test book and rebuild on every change:

The -- is required so the flags reach the script rather than npm. Useful flags: -t (one target; -l lists them), -o (output directory), -w (watch and rebuild), and -c (a JSON string of options and variables, the same ones a publisher sets, for trying colors and options). Run npm run build -- -h for the full list; details are in script/cssbuilder/README.md.

The files in css/dist are checked into the repository so the common themes work without a build. Regenerating them is the committer's responsibility, usually a separate dedicated commit. If your contribution changes SCSS source you do not need to rebuild and commit css/distjust make sure your source compiles.

section.label: css-making-changes
section.title: Making a Change
section.p:
A reliable way to locate the source for something you see on a page:
Inspect the element with your browser's developer tools and read its class names styles by class, not by tag.
Search the css tree for that class to find the rule, usually in a components file (shared) or a targets file (one theme).
Decide which of the four mechanisms in fits: a value change is probably a CSS or SCSS variable; a structural change may need a theme-owned rule or a variant file.
Build that one target into a test book with -o and -w, reload, and iterate. Check dark mode and a narrow window, and test other targets if you touched a shared component.
Follow the usual conventions when submitting ( and ), and raise larger ideas, such as a brand-new theme, on the pretext-dev discussion group first.
